item.title: A World Cup Like No Other Comes to North America
item.body:
## The Biggest Tournament Soccer Has Ever Staged

This summer, the FIFA World Cup is being co-hosted by the United States, Canada, and Mexico — and it is rewriting the tournament's scale. For the first time, **48 national teams** are competing, spread across 16 host cities on three borders.

### Why This Edition Is Different

- **More teams, more nations.** The expanded field means countries rarely seen on the World Cup stage get their moment, broadening the map of who can dream of going deep.
- **Three host countries.** From Mexico City's historic Estadio Azteca to stadiums across the U.S. and Canada, the tournament stretches across an entire continent.
- **A new format.** A larger bracket reshapes the math of qualifying, survival, and the long road to the final.

### What to Watch For

As the group stage gives way to the knockout rounds, the tournament tightens into its most dramatic phase — single matches where a season of preparation can turn on one moment. The traditional powers carry pressure and pedigree, while smaller nations arrive with nothing to lose, the exact recipe that produces World Cup upsets.

### Beyond the Pitch

For host cities, this is more than sport. It is a summer of full stadiums, packed fan zones, and a global audience pointed at North America. For casual U.S. fans, it is also an invitation: a chance to learn the rhythms of a game that the rest of the planet has loved for generations.

**The stage is set.** Whoever lifts the trophy, this expanded, continent-spanning World Cup is already a milestone — the moment soccer's grandest event grew to its largest size yet.
